aspirin.name: Aspirin
aspirin.description: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug and antiplatelet agent used for pain, fever, and cardiovascular protection.

ibuprofen.name: Ibuprofen
ibuprofen.description: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug used for pain relief, fever reduction, and inflammation.

warfarin.name: Warfarin
warfarin.description: Anticoagulant that inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Narrow therapeutic index.

metformin.name: Metformin
metformin.description: First-line medication for type 2 diabetes. Decreases hepatic glucose production.

lisinopril.name: Lisinopril
lisinopril.description: ACE inhibitor used for hypertension, heart failure, and post-myocardial infarction.

atorvastatin.name: Atorvastatin
atorvastatin.description: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor used to lower cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk.

omeprazole.name: Omeprazole
omeprazole.description: Proton pump inhibitor that reduces stomach acid. Used for GERD, ulcers, and acid-related conditions.

amlodipine.name: Amlodipine
amlodipine.description: Calcium channel blocker used for hypertension and angina.

metoprolol.name: Metoprolol
metoprolol.description: Selective beta-1 adrenergic blocker used for hypertension, angina, and heart failure.

simvastatin.name: Simvastatin
simvastatin.description: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor used to lower cholesterol. Metabolized extensively by CYP3A4.

clopidogrel.name: Clopidogrel
clopidogrel.description: Antiplatelet agent that inhibits ADP-induced platelet aggregation.

fluconazole.name: Fluconazole
fluconazole.description: Triazole antifungal that is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 enzymes.

ciprofloxacin.name: Ciprofloxacin
ciprofloxacin.description: Broad-spectrum antibiotic. Potent inhibitor of CYP1A2.

acetaminophen.name: Acetaminophen
acetaminophen.description: Common analgesic and antipyretic. Hepatotoxic metabolite NAPQI formed via CYP2E1.

losartan.name: Losartan
losartan.description: Angiotensin II receptor blocker used for hypertension and diabetic nephropathy.

hydrochlorothiazide.name: Hydrochlorothiazide
hydrochlorothiazide.description: Thiazide diuretic used for hypertension and edema. Increases lithium and potassium loss.

gabapentin.name: Gabapentin
gabapentin.description: Used for neuropathic pain and seizures. Not metabolized by CYP enzymes. Renally excreted.

prednisone.name: Prednisone
prednisone.description: Synthetic corticosteroid used for inflammation and immune suppression. Metabolized by CYP3A4.

diclofenac.name: Diclofenac
diclofenac.description: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug. Metabolized primarily by CYP2C9.

pantoprazole.name: Pantoprazole
pantoprazole.description: Proton pump inhibitor with less CYP interaction potential than omeprazole.

CYP3A4.name: CYP3A4
CYP3A4.description: The most abundant CYP enzyme, metabolizes ~50% of all drugs. Located primarily in liver and intestine.
CYP3A4.risk_note: When multiple CYP3A4 substrates are combined with an inhibitor, drug levels can rise dangerously.

CYP2C9.name: CYP2C9
CYP2C9.description: Metabolizes ~15% of drugs including NSAIDs and warfarin. Genetic polymorphisms common.
CYP2C9.risk_note: CYP2C9 inhibition can cause warfarin toxicity leading to severe bleeding.

CYP2C19.name: CYP2C19
CYP2C19.description: Metabolizes PPIs and clopidogrel. Highly polymorphic — poor metabolizers at elevated risk.
CYP2C19.risk_note: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, reducing clopidogrel activation and antiplatelet efficacy.

CYP2D6.name: CYP2D6
CYP2D6.description: Metabolizes ~25% of drugs including beta-blockers, antidepressants, and opioids.
CYP2D6.risk_note: CYP2D6 poor metabolizers accumulate metoprolol, causing excessive beta-blockade.

CYP1A2.name: CYP1A2
CYP1A2.description: Metabolizes caffeine, theophylline, and some drugs. Induced by smoking.
CYP1A2.risk_note: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, increasing warfarin and theophylline levels.

CYP2E1.name: CYP2E1
CYP2E1.description: Metabolizes acetaminophen to toxic NAPQI. Induced by chronic alcohol use.
CYP2E1.risk_note: CYP2E1 induction (e.g., by alcohol) increases NAPQI production, raising hepatotoxicity risk.

aspirin+warfarin.mechanism: Both drugs inhibit hemostasis. Aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation via COX-1 while warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Combined use synergistically increases bleeding risk.

aspirin+ibuprofen.mechanism: Ibuprofen competitively inhibits COX-1 binding, blocking aspirin's irreversible antiplatelet effect. Concurrent NSAID use dramatically increases GI bleeding risk.

aspirin+clopidogrel.mechanism: Dual antiplatelet therapy — both inhibit platelet function through different mechanisms (COX-1 vs ADP receptor). Synergistic bleeding risk.

warfarin+ibuprofen.mechanism: Ibuprofen displaces warfarin from plasma protein binding and inhibits CYP2C9-mediated warfarin metabolism, increasing free warfarin concentration.

warfarin+fluconazole.mechanism: Fluconazole is a potent CYP2C9 inhibitor, dramatically reducing warfarin clearance. Can cause 2-3x increase in warfarin levels.

warfarin+omeprazole.mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 and weakly inhibits CYP2C9, modestly reducing warfarin metabolism.

warfarin+acetaminophen.mechanism: Acetaminophen's metabolite inhibits vitamin K cycle enzymes, potentiating warfarin's anticoagulant effect. Risk increases with doses >2g/day for >3 days.

warfarin+ciprofloxacin.mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2-mediated warfarin metabolism and kills vitamin K-producing gut flora, amplifying anticoagulant effect.

atorvastatin+fluconazole.mechanism: Fluconazole strongly inhibits CYP3A4 which metabolizes atorvastatin, causing statin accumulation and risk of severe muscle breakdown.

simvastatin+amlodipine.mechanism: Amlodipine inhibits CYP3A4-mediated simvastatin metabolism. FDA recommends limiting simvastatin to 20mg with amlodipine.

simvastatin+fluconazole.mechanism: Potent CYP3A4 inhibition by fluconazole causes massive simvastatin accumulation. Contraindicated combination.

omeprazole+clopidogrel.mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, which is required to convert clopidogrel (prodrug) into its active metabolite. Reduces antiplatelet efficacy by 40-50%.

metformin+hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Thiazide diuretics can increase blood glucose, opposing metformin's glucose-lowering effect. Dehydration increases lactic acidosis risk.

lisinopril+losartan.mechanism: Dual RAAS blockade (ACE inhibitor + ARB) causes excessive suppression of aldosterone, leading to dangerous potassium elevation and renal failure.

lisinopril+hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Common therapeutic combination. HCTZ offsets hyperkalemia from lisinopril. Monitor electrolytes.

metoprolol+amlodipine.mechanism: Both drugs reduce heart rate and blood pressure through different mechanisms. Additive cardiodepressant effect.

acetaminophen+warfarin.mechanism: Acetaminophen metabolite NAPQI interferes with vitamin K cycle. Effect dose-dependent — significant above 2g/day.

prednisone+aspirin.mechanism: Corticosteroids reduce gastric mucosal protection while aspirin inhibits COX-mediated prostaglandin synthesis. Synergistic GI toxicity.

prednisone+metformin.mechanism: Corticosteroids increase hepatic gluconeogenesis and induce insulin resistance, directly opposing metformin's mechanism of action.

ciprofloxacin+acetaminophen.mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, slightly reducing acetaminophen metabolism. Clinically significant only at high acetaminophen doses.

diclofenac+warfarin.mechanism: Diclofenac is metabolized by CYP2C9 like warfarin. Competitive inhibition increases warfarin levels. NSAID GI toxicity compounds bleeding risk.

diclofenac+lisinopril.mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow regulation, reducing ACE inhibitor efficacy and increasing nephrotoxicity risk.

gabapentin+metoprolol.mechanism: Additive CNS depression. Both can cause dizziness. No significant pharmacokinetic interaction.

atorvastatin+omeprazole.mechanism: Omeprazole is a weak CYP3A4 inhibitor. May modestly increase atorvastatin exposure. Usually clinically insignificant.

atorvastatin+amlodipine.mechanism: Amlodipine weakly inhibits CYP3A4. FDA allows full-dose atorvastatin with amlodipine, unlike simvastatin.

metformin+lisinopril.mechanism: ACE inhibitors may enhance insulin sensitivity, slightly potentiating metformin. Generally safe therapeutic combination.

aspirin+warfarin+ibuprofen.mechanism: Triple threat: aspirin + ibuprofen provide dual COX inhibition destroying GI mucosal protection, while warfarin prevents clot formation. All three compete for CYP2C9 metabolism. This combination has caused documented fatalities.

warfarin+fluconazole+aspirin.mechanism: Fluconazole potently inhibits CYP2C9, causing 2-3x warfarin accumulation. Aspirin adds antiplatelet effect. This triple combination creates extreme, uncontrollable bleeding risk.

simvastatin+amlodipine+fluconazole.mechanism: Both amlodipine and fluconazole inhibit CYP3A4 which metabolizes simvastatin. Dual CYP3A4 inhibition causes massive statin accumulation, leading to muscle breakdown and kidney damage from myoglobin.

omeprazole+clopidogrel+aspirin.mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19-mediated clopidogrel activation. With reduced antithrombotic protection from both clopidogrel and aspirin's interaction, patients on dual antiplatelet therapy face increased risk of cardiovascular events.

lisinopril+losartan+hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Dual RAAS blockade (ACE inhibitor + ARB) with a diuretic causes unpredictable electrolyte shifts. Severe hyperkalemia can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias.

prednisone+aspirin+ibuprofen.mechanism: Triple assault on GI mucosa: prednisone reduces mucosal defense, aspirin and ibuprofen both inhibit protective prostaglandin synthesis. Risk of GI perforation significantly elevated.

metformin+prednisone+hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Both prednisone (gluconeogenesis induction) and HCTZ (pancreatic beta-cell suppression) oppose metformin's glucose-lowering effect. Triple combination can cause loss of glycemic control.

warfarin+ciprofloxacin+aspirin.mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2-mediated warfarin metabolism AND kills vitamin K-producing gut bacteria. Combined with aspirin's antiplatelet effect, creates extreme bleeding risk.

atorvastatin+amlodipine+omeprazole.mechanism: Multiple weak CYP3A4 inhibitors (amlodipine + omeprazole) combined can have additive effect on atorvastatin metabolism, increasing statin exposure beyond what either alone would cause.

metoprolol+amlodipine+lisinopril.mechanism: Triple antihypertensive therapy: beta-blocker + calcium channel blocker + ACE inhibitor. Additive hypotensive effect can cause dangerous blood pressure drops, especially in elderly patients.

diclofenac+lisinopril+metformin.mechanism: NSAID reduces renal blood flow, impairing lisinopril efficacy and metformin clearance. Renally impaired metformin elimination increases lactic acidosis risk.

acetaminophen+warfarin+ciprofloxacin.mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 (warfarin metabolism), acetaminophen interferes with vitamin K cycle, and all three drugs undergo hepatic processing. Triple hepatic stress with anticoagulation amplification.

aspirin.notes: Take with food to reduce GI irritation. If on anticoagulants, maximum 8-hour gap.

ibuprofen.notes: Take at least 8 hours apart from aspirin. 30 min before or 8 hours after.

warfarin.notes: Take at same time every day. Avoid major dietary vitamin K changes.

metformin.notes: Take with food to reduce GI side effects. Extended-release: once daily with evening meal.

lisinopril.notes: Once daily, consistent time. May cause dizziness initially.

atorvastatin.notes: Cholesterol synthesis peaks at night. Does not need to be taken with food.

omeprazole.notes: Most effective when taken before first meal. Take 12+ hours before clopidogrel if both needed.

amlodipine.notes: Long half-life allows flexible dosing. Avoid grapefruit (CYP3A4).

metoprolol.notes: Take with food to increase absorption. Never stop abruptly — taper over 1-2 weeks.

simvastatin.notes: Must be taken in evening for optimal effect. Avoid grapefruit juice.

clopidogrel.notes: Take 12+ hours apart from omeprazole if needed. Pantoprazole preferred over omeprazole.

fluconazole.notes: Space from statins by 12+ hours. Monitor liver function.

ciprofloxacin.notes: Avoid antacids, dairy, and supplements within 2 hours. Hydrate well.

acetaminophen.notes: Limit to 2g/day if taking warfarin. Space doses 4-6 hours apart.

losartan.notes: Once or twice daily. Do not combine with ACE inhibitors.

hydrochlorothiazide.notes: Take in morning to avoid nocturia. Monitor potassium levels.

gabapentin.notes: Space doses evenly (approx 8 hours). Can be taken with or without food.

prednisone.notes: Take with food to reduce GI effects. Morning dosing mimics natural cortisol rhythm.

diclofenac.notes: Take with food. Avoid concurrent aspirin or other NSAIDs.

pantoprazole.notes: Preferred PPI with clopidogrel (less CYP2C19 interaction than omeprazole).

Gastrointestinal bleeding.description: Bleeding in the stomach or intestines, can be life-threatening

GI ulceration.description: Open sores in the stomach or duodenal lining

GI perforation.description: Hole in the wall of the GI tract — surgical emergency

Liver toxicity.description: Drug-induced liver injury from hepatotoxic metabolites

Hepatotoxicity.description: Liver damage from drug metabolism overload

Rhabdomyolysis.description: Breakdown of muscle tissue releasing myoglobin, can cause kidney failure

Hyperkalemia.description: Dangerously high potassium — can cause cardiac arrest

Elevated INR.description: International Normalized Ratio above therapeutic range

Hemorrhage.description: Massive blood loss from internal or external source

Intracranial hemorrhage.description: Bleeding inside the skull — life-threatening emergency

Syncope.description: Fainting due to reduced brain perfusion

Lactic acidosis.description: Dangerous buildup of lactic acid — medical emergency
